Article List
Date
Source Code
---
title: Dec Measurement System
description: >
  Introducing Dec, a measurement system,
  which uses turns instead of months, weeks,
  hours, minutes, seconds, and degrees.
image: /asset/colorWheelCompass.svg
url: https://maptv.github.io/dec
decdate: 2026+096
path: /dec
slug: dec
aliases:
  - /d
engine: knitr
fig-cap-location: bottom
---

:::{#firstnav}
{{< include /asset/_decnav.qmd >}}
:::

# Dec measurement system {.hiddenheading #sec-dec}

This part of my website focuses on Dec, a [measurement system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System_of_units_of_measurement#:~:text=a%20collection%20of%20units%20of%20measurement%20and%20rules%20relating%20them%20to%20each%20other) that [I](https://maptv.github.io) created. All Dec measurements are based on [turns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turn_%28angle%29#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20plane%20angle%20measurement%20equal%20to%202%CF%80%C2%A0radians%2C%20360%C2%A0degrees). When measuring [angles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angle#:~:text=the%20figure%20formed%20by%20two%20rays)📐, a [turn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turn_%28angle%29#:~:text=the%20Greek%20letter,to%20one%20turn) ([t](#t){#turn .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="turn"}) represents a full⭕️circle and equals 360 [degrees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Degree_(angle)#:~:text=a%20measurement%20of%20a%20plane%20angle%20in%20which%20one%20full%20rotation%20is%20360%20degrees) ([°](#deg){#degree .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"}) or [$\underline\tau$](#2pi){#tau .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tau"}, 2[𝜋](#pi){#taudividedby2 .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tau"}, or 900[ϡ](#sampi){#taudividedby900 .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="sampi"} [radians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radian#:~:text=the%20unit%20of%20angle%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units) ([rad](#rad){#radian .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="radians"}). [Geographic coordinates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system#:~:text=positions%20directly%20on%20Earth%20as%20latitude%20and%20longitude) and [compass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compass#:~:text=a%20device%20that%20shows%20the%20cardinal%20directions%20used%20for%20navigation%20and%20geographic%20orientation)🧭directions are angles and thus can --- and should --- be measured in turns instead of [rad](#rad){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="radians"} or [°](#deg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"}.

# Longitude latitude course {.hiddenheading #sec-llc}

Dec measures [longitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Longitude#:~:text=denoted%20by%20the%20Greek%20letter%20lambda) in [wěi](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%B7%AF#:~:text=(geography)-,latitude,-coordinate%20terms%C2%A0%E2%96%B2) ([w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal lines on a map"}), [latitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latitude#:~:text=denoted%20by%20the%20Greek%20lower%2Dcase%20letter%20phi) in [meridians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meridian_arc#Full_meridian_(polar_perimeter):~:text=The%20polar%20Earth%27s%20circumference%20is%20simply%20four%20times%20quarter%20meridian) ([m](#m){#meridian .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridians"}), and compass directions in [roses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compass_rose#:~:text=a%20polar%20diagram%20displaying%20the%20orientation%20of%20the%20cardinal%20directions)🌹([r](#r){#rose .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="roses"}). To measure certain kinds of angles, Dec uses specific types of turns with distinct names like [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal line on a map"}, [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridian"}, or [r](#r){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="rose"}. All turn types can be combined with [metric prefixes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metric_prefix#:~:text=a%20unit%20prefix%20that%20precedes%20a%20basic%20unit%20of%20measure%20to%20indicate%20a%20multiple%20or%20submultiple%20of%20the%20unit), like [deci](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deci-#:~:text=a%20decimal%20unit%20prefix%20in%20the%20metric%20system%20denoting%20a%20factor%20of%20one%20tenth), [centi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centi-#:~:text=a%20unit%20prefix%20in%20the%20metric%20system%20denoting%20a%20factor%20of%20one%20hundredth), or [milli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milli-#:~:text=a%20unit%20prefix%20in%20the%20metric%20system%20denoting%20a%20factor%20of%20one%20thousandth), to create turn [submultiples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiple_%28mathematics%29#:~:text=of%20%22a%20being-,a%20unit%20fraction,-of%20b%22%20%28a), such as [deciturns]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a turn"} ([dt](#dt){#deciturn .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="deciturns"}), [centiturns]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a turn"} ([ct](#ct){#centiturn .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centiturns"}), or [milliturns]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a turn"} ([mt](#mt){#milliturn .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliturns"}).

@tbl-map below⬇️provides the current longitude in [milliwěi]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a wěi"} ([mw](#mw){#milliwei .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"}) and latitude in [millimeridians]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a meridian"} ([mm](#mm){#millimeridian .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) of Points [0]{.point0} and [1]{.point1} on the [map](#fig-distmap)🗺️beneath @tbl-map. By default, Point [0]{.point0} is at [800]{.color8} [mw](#mw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"} and [0]{.color0} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}, near the [Galápagos🏝️archipelago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands#:~:text=an%20archipelago%20of%20volcanic%20islands%20in%20the%20Eastern%20Pacific) of Ecuador🇪🇨, and Point [1]{.point1} is at [800]{.color8} [mw](#mw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"} and [100]{.color1} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}, near the bottom of the [Missouri bootheel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri_Bootheel#:~:text=a%20salient%20(protrusion)%20located%20in%20the%20southeasternmost%20part%20of%20the%20U.S.%20state%20of%20Missouri) in the United States🇺🇸.

To move the points, click the [map](#fig-distmap) or edit their coordinates in @tbl-map. The [toggle](https://observablehq.com/framework/inputs/toggle)✅inputs above⬆️@tbl-map add layers to the [map](#fig-distmap): country borders, a rainbow🌈colored grid of Dec [graticules](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graticule_(cartography)#:~:text=a%20graphical%20depiction%20of%20a%20coordinate%20system%20as%20a%20grid%20of%20lines), a [choropleth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Choropleth_map#:~:text=a%20type%20of%20statistical%20thematic%20map%20that%20uses%20pseudocolor) of [Coordinated Universal Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time#:~:text=the%20primary%20time%20standard%20globally%20used%20to%20regulate%20clocks%20and%20time) ([utc](#utc){#coordinateduniversaltime .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="Coordinated Universal Time"}) time zones, and [solar terminator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terminator_(solar)#:~:text=a%20moving%20line%20that%20divides%20the%20daylit%20side%20and%20the%20dark%20night%20side%20of%20a%20planetary%20body) shading with a yellow🟡dot denoting the [point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subsolar_point#:~:text=the%20point%20at%20which%20its%20Sun%20is%20perceived%20to%20be%20directly%20overhead) where the Sun☀️is [directly overhead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zenith#:~:text=the%20imaginary%20point%20on%20the%20celestial%20sphere%20directly%20%22above%22%20a%20particular%20location): \${sunLonHsl} [mw](#mw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"} and \${sunLatHsl} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}.

Alongside the geographic coordinates of a point, each row of @tbl-map contains the [course](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Azimuth#:~:text=%20azimuth%20is%20usually%20denoted%20alpha) in [milliroses]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a compass rose"} ([mr](#mr){#millirose .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"}) we would need to maintain to travel🧳the shortest distance to the other point. The shortest distance is shown as orange🟠dots on the [map](#fig-distmap). The default courses in [mr](#mr){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"} are [0]{.color0} (North) from Point [0]{.point0} to [1]{.point1} and [500]{.color5} (South) from Point [1]{.point1} to [0]{.point0}.

# Distance speed duration {.hiddenheading #sec-dsd}

Dec measures distance in [taurs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turn_(angle)#Tau_proposals:~:text=%E2%81%A0%20turn-,Circumference%20of%20a%20circle,-%F0%9D%90%B6) ([c](#c){#taur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taurs"}), speed in [omegars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_velocity#:~:text=linear%20velocity%20is%20the%20radius%20times%20the%20angular%20velocity) ([v](#v){#omegar .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"}), and time in years ([y](#y){#year .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="years"}) and days ([d](#d){#day .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days"}). Each of these four turn types approximates ($\approx$) a physical property of the Earth🌍: [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} = [$\underline{\tau r}$](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} $\approx$ its [circumference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference#:~:text=the%20distance%20around%20Earth), [y](#y){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"} $\approx$ the duration of its [orbit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_orbit#:~:text=From%20a%20vantage%20point%20above%20the%20north%20pole%20of%20either%20the%20Sun%20or%20Earth%2C%20Earth%20would%20appear%20to%20revolve%20in%20a%20counterclockwise%20direction%20around%20the%20Sun) around the Sun️, [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day"} $\approx$ the duration of its [rotation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation#:~:text=the%20rotation%20of%20planet%20Earth%20around%20its%20own%20axis) on its [axis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axial_tilt#:~:text=the%20imaginary%20line%20that%20passes%20through%20both%20the%20north%20pole%20and%20south%20pole), and $\text c\over\text d$ = [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegar"} = [$\underline{\omega r}$](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegar"} $\approx$ the speed of its rotation at the [Equator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equator#:~:text=the%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20divides%20Earth%20into%20the%20Northern%20and%20Southern%20hemispheres).

At a speed of [0.5]{.color5} [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} or [500]{.color5} [milliomegars]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of an omegar"} ([mv](#mv){#milliomegar .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"}), we could travel the [0.1]{.color1} [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taurs"} or [100]{.color1} [millitaurs]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a taur"} ([mc](#mc){#millitaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millitaurs"}) between the default positions📍of Points [0]{.point0} and [1]{.point1} in [0.2]{.color2} [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days"} or [200]{.color2} [millidays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a day"} ([md](#md){#milliday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"}). The time required to travel between two points is the distance divided by the speed: \${distance_mcHsl0} [mc](#mc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millitaurs"} &divide; \${velocity_vHsl0} [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} = \${traveltimeHsl0} [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"} = \${distance_cHsl} [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taurs"} &divide; \${velocity_vHsl1} [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} = \${distance_mcHsl1} [mc](#mc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millitaurs"} &divide; \${velocity_mvHsl} [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"} = \${traveltimeHsl1} [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days"}.

## Interactive world map {.hiddenheading .unnumbered #map}

{{< include /dec/_maptable.qmd >}}

## Color wheel compass {.hiddenheading .unnumbered #cwc}

{{< include /dec/_color.qmd >}}

The [color🎨wheel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_wheel#:~:text=an%20abstract%20illustrative%20organization%20of%20color%20hues%20around%20a%20circle) compass above indicates both a [hue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hue#:~:text=an%20angular%20position%20around%20a%20central%20or%20neutral%20point%20or%20axis%20on%20a%20color%20space%20coordinate%20diagram) in [mt](#mt){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliturns"} and a course in [mr](#mr){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"}. We can convert the hue to [HSL and HSV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HSL_and_HSV#:~:text=the%20two%20most%20common%20cylindrical%2Dcoordinate%20representations%20of%20points%20in%20an%20RGB%20color%20model) degrees ([h°](#hdeg){#huedegree .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="HSL or HSV degrees"}) and the course to compass degrees ([c°](#cdeg){#compassdegree .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="compass degrees"}): 25 [mr](#mr){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"} = 9 [c°](#cdeg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="compass degrees"}. To rotate🔄the color wheel compass, use the "Hue" [range](https://observablehq.com/framework/inputs/range)🎚️and [hue bar](https://observablehq.com/@paavanb/progressive-color-picker) inputs beneath it or change the course from Point [0]{.point0} to [1]{.point1} by interacting with @tbl-map or the [map](#fig-distmap) above.

# Red green blue (rgb) {.hiddenheading #sec-rgb}

Beneath the hue bar, @tbl-color compares the current Point [0]{.point0} to [1]{.point1} course in its top row with the [cardinal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardinal_direction#:~:text=north%2C%20south%2C%20east%2C%20and%20west) and [intercardinal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardinal_direction#:~:text=northeast%20(NE)%2C%20southeast%20(SE)%2C%20southwest%20(SW)%2C%20and%20northwest%20(NW)) directions. Together, the range inputs underneath the hue bar form a "hue saturation lightness" ([hsl](#hsl){#huesaturationlightness .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hue saturation lightness"}) triplet. Like "[[red]{.color0} [green]{.color4} [blue]{.color7}](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RGB_color_model#:~:text=an%20additive%20color%20model)" ([rgb](#rgb){#redgreenblue .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="red green blue"}) or [hexadecimal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_colors#Hex_triplet:~:text=hexadecimal%20number%20used%20in%20HTML%2C%20CSS%2C%20SVG%2C%20and%20other%20computing%20applications%20to%20represent%20colors) ([hex](#hex){#hexadecimal .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexadecimal"}) triplets, [hsl](#hsl){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hue saturation lightness"} triplets specify a full-fledged color instead of just a hue.

Color labels🏷️provide a general idea of angular [measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angle#:~:text=The%20magnitude%20of%20an%20angle), regardless of the metric prefixes or [units](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angle#Units) we use. Therefore, we can reuse♻️colors across many different contexts. Most often, [red]{.color0} designates starting points, like North ([0]{.color0} [mr](#mr){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"}) and [Longitude 0](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/18th_meridian_west#:~:text=a%20line%20of%20longitude%20that%20extends%20from%20the%20North%20Pole%20across%20the%20Arctic%20Ocean%2C%20Greenland%2C%20Iceland%2C%20the%20Atlantic%20Ocean%2C%20the%20Canary%20Islands%2C%20the%20Southern%20Ocean%2C%20and%20Antarctica%20to%20the%20South%20Pole) ([0]{.color0} [mw](#mw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"}), and [cyan]{.color5} denotes midpoints, such as South ([500]{.color5} [mr](#mr){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliroses"}) and [Longitude 5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/162nd_meridian_east#:~:text=a%20line%20of%20longitude%20that%20extends%20from%20the%20North%20Pole%20across%20the%20Arctic%20Ocean%2C%20Asia%2C%20the%20Pacific%20Ocean%2C%20the%20Southern%20Ocean%2C%20and%20Antarctica%20to%20the%20South%20Pole) ([500]{.color5} [mw](#mw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a horizontal line on a map"}).

::: {.callout-warning}
# Bad Pun Alert
Feeling ***disoriented***😵‍💫? Of [***course***](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Course_(navigation)#:~:text=the%20cardinal%20direction%20in%20which%20the%20craft%20is%20to%20be%20steered) you are! Color labels️ can help you find your [***bearings***](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bearing_(navigation)#:~:text=the%20horizontal%20angle%20between%20the%20direction%20of%20an%20object%20and%20north%20or%20another%20object), stay on [***track***](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Course_(navigation)#:~:text=The%20path%20that%20a%20vessel%20follows), and avoid [***heading***](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Course_(navigation)#:~:text=the%20direction%20where%20the%20watercraft's%20bow%20or%20the%20aircraft's%20nose%20is%20pointed) aches🤕. [Orange]{.orange} you glad I couldn't think of a color pun?
:::

The Equator ([0]{.color0} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) is the [major latitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_of_latitude#:~:text=mark%20the%20divisions%20between%20the%20five%20principal%20geographical%20zones) midway between the South ([-250]{.color750} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) and North ([250]{.color250} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) Poles. Unlike the Equator, the Tropics of [Cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer#:~:text=northernmost%20circle%20of%20latitude%20where%20the%20Sun%20can%20be%20seen%20directly%20overhead)♋([65]{.color065} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) and [Capricorn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn#:~:text=the%20southernmost%20latitude%20where%20the%20Sun%20can%20be%20seen%20directly%20overhead)♑️([-65]{.color935} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) and the [Arctic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arctic_Circle#:~:text=the%20southernmost%20latitude%20at%20which%2C%20on%20the%20winter%20solstice%20in%20the%20Northern%20Hemisphere%2C%20the%20Sun%20does%20not%20rise%20all%20day%2C%20and%20on%20the%20Northern%20Hemisphere%27s%20summer%20solstice%2C%20the%20Sun%20does%20not%20set) ([250]{.color250} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"} -- [65]{.color065} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"} = [185]{.color185} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) and [Antarctic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctic_Circle#:~:text=the%20Sun%20is%20above%20the%20horizon%20for%2024%20continuous%20hours%20at%20least%20once%20per%20year%20(and%20therefore%20visible%20at%20solar%20midnight)%20and%20the%20centre%20of%20the%20Sun%20(ignoring%20refraction)%20is%20below%20the%20horizon%20for%2024%20continuous%20hours%20at%20least%20once%20per%20year%20(and%20therefore%20not%20visible%20at%20solar%20noon)) ([65]{.color065} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"} -- [250]{.color250} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"} = [-185]{.color815} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) Circles are defined by the [axial tilt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axial_tilt#Earth:~:text=the%20angle%20between%20the%20ecliptic%20and%20the%20celestial%20equator%20on%20the%20celestial%20sphere) of the Earth🌏: [65]{.color065} [mt](#mt){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliturns"}.

# Dec time zones {.hiddenheading #sec-dtz}

Enable the [“Grid” toggle input](#toggles) to see Latitudes [-2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/72nd_parallel_south#:~:text=a%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20is%2072%20degrees%20south%20of%20the%20Earth's%20equatorial%20plane%20in%20the%20Antarctic) ([-200]{.color8} [mm]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}), [-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/36th_parallel_south#:~:text=a%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20is%2036%20degrees%20south%20of%20the%20Earth's%20equatorial%20plane) ([-100]{.color9} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}), [0](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equator#:~:text=the%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20divides%20Earth%20into%20the%20Northern%20and%20Southern%20hemispheres) ([0]{.color0} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}), [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/36th_parallel_north#:~:text=a%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20is%2036%20degrees%20north%20of%20the%20Earth's%20equatorial%20plane) ([100]{.color1} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}), and [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/72nd_parallel_north#:~:text=a%20circle%20of%20latitude%20that%20is%2072%20degrees%20north%20of%20the%20Earth's%20equatorial%20plane%2C%20in%20the%20Arctic) ([200]{.color2} [mm](#mm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millimeridians"}) on the [map](#fig-distmap) above along with the ten major longitudes that divide the Earth🌎into the ten Dec time zones. Notably, Longitude [0]{.color0} is the major longitude that functions as both the [Prime Meridian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prime_meridian#:~:text=an%20arbitrarily%2Dchosen%20meridian%20%28a%20line%20of%20longitude%29%20in%20a%20geographic%20coordinate%20system%20at%20which%20longitude%20is%20defined%20to%20be%200%C2%B0) and [International Date Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Date_Line#:~:text=the%20line%20between%20the%20South%20and%20North%20Poles%20that%20is%20the%20boundary%20between%20one%20calendar%20day%20and%20the%20next) in Dec.

Like the ten major longitudes that separate them, Dec time zones are numbered [0]{.color0} to [9]{.color9}. Based on its current [deciwěi]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenth of a wěi"} ([dw](#dw){#deciwei .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a horizontal line on a map"}) longitude, \${point0lHsl}, Point [0]{.point0} on the [map](#fig-distmap) above is in Zone \${point0zHsl}. The number assigned to each time zone is its offset from Zone [0]{.color0} in [decidays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a day"} ([dd](#dd){#deciday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decidays"}). To obtain the [dd](#dd){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="deciday"} offset at a location, we [floor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floor_and_ceiling_functions#:~:text=the%20greatest%20integer%20less%20than%20or%20equal%20to%20x) its [dw](#dw){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a horizontal line on a map"} longitude: ⌊\${decLonHsl}⌋ = \${decZonHsl}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| class: slider
//| label: loninput
viewof longitude = Inputs.range([0, 10], {label: "Longitude", value: .5, step: .01})
```

Each Dec time zone is 0.1 [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="of a horizontal line on a map"} wide and 0.5 [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridians"} long. While 1 [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridian"} is always [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1 [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} long, the length of a [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal line on a map"} [varies by latitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Longitude#Length_of_a_degree_of_longitude:~:text=depends%20only%20on%20the%20radius%20of%20a%20circle%20of%20latitude). At the Equator, 1 [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal line on a map"} is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1 [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} long. At the [North](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Pole#:~:text=the%20point%20in%20the%20Northern%20Hemisphere%20where%20the%20Earth%27s%20axis%20of%20rotation%20meets%20its%20surface) or [South](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Pole#:~:text=the%20point%20in%20the%20Southern%20Hemisphere%20where%20the%20Earth%27s%20axis%20of%20rotation%20meets%20its%20surface) Pole, the length of a [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal line on a map"} is zero. The approximate [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} length of a [w](#w){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="horizontal line on a map"} is the [cosine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine_and_cosine#:~:text=the%20ratio%20of%20the%20length%20of%20the%20adjacent%20leg%20to%20that%20of%20the%20hypotenuse) of its latitude in [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridians"}, [rad](#rad){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="radians"}, or [°](#deg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"}, depending on the input requirement of our cosine function: cos(\${parLat}\${conversionFactor}) = \${parLen}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| class: slider
//| label: latinput
viewof latitude = Inputs.range([-.25, .25], {label: "Latitude", value: 0, step: .001})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| class: radiobuttons
//| label: costype
viewof costype = Inputs.radio(["turns", "radians", "degrees"], {label: "Cosine input", value: "turns"})
```

# Zone equatorial meter (zem) {.hiddenheading #sec-zem}

:::{.column-page-right fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;font-size:.825rem;" id="zemmodulor" .lighthouse}
![](../asset/Modulor_measurements.svg){#zModu}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Modulor_measurements.svg#mw-jump-to-license)
:::

Apart from [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"}, Dec also measures distance using a unit called the **z**one **e**quatorial **m**eter (zem). The width of a Dec time zone at the Equator is approximately ten million ([~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"}) zem ([z](#z){#zoneequatorialmeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}). Similarly, the distance from the Equator to one of the Poles is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} [meters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metre#Definition:~:text=the%20base%20unit%20of%20length%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units). In other words, a [decimeridian]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a meridian"} ([dm](#dm){#decimeridian .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decimeridian"}) is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} long and a [quarter meridian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meridian_arc#Full_meridian_(polar_perimeter):~:text=The%20distance%20from%20the%20equator%20to%20the%20pole) is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} meters long.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" id="zemhands" .hand}
![](../asset/hands.svg)
[[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Typing-colour_for-finger-positions.svg)]{.handlabel}
:::

# Length area volume {.hiddenheading #sec-lav}

You can approximate a [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} using your hands🤲. With your palms flat on a table in front of you and the tips of your thumbs👍touching, the maximum distance between the tips of your pinkies is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}. When you spread out the fingers on one hand✋or do the "[call me](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=the%20gesture%20is%20commonly%20understood%20to%20mimic%20the%20handset%20of%20a%20traditional%20landline%20telephone)", "[drink](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=placing%20the%20thumb%20to%20the%20mouth%20and%20motioning%20the%20little%20finger%20upward%20as%20if%20tipping%20up%20a%20bottle%27s%20bottom%20end)", or "[shaka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=a%20gesture%20with%20friendly%20intent%20often%20associated%20with%20Hawaii%20and%20surf%20culture)"🤙gesture, your thumb and pinky tips are [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.5 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} apart.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" #zemarms}
![](../asset/squareZem.svg){#zArms}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Extended_arm.jpg){#armlabel}
:::

To visualize a square [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} ([z&sup2;](#z2){#squarezem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"}), imagine four people standing in a circle, facing inward, each with their right hand placed on top of the elbow of the person to their right. Alternatively, two people can stand in front of each other and raise their arms💪, placing one hand on the elbow of the other person and the other hand on their own elbow.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" #zemlift}
![](../asset/Man_Lifting_Barbell_Cartoon.svg){#zLift}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Man_Lifting_Barbell_Cartoon.svg){#liftlabel}
:::

You can approximate a [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} yourself by sitting in a chair🪑or standing🧍with your knees and feet🦶1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, 4 [decimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a meter"}, or 16 inches apart, which is probably about the width of your hips or shoulders. The [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} will be between your shins, its top will be below your knees, and its bottom will be either above your ankles or feet, depending on your height.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" id="zemcubic"}
{{< include _cubic.qmd >}}
[Dimensions.com](https://www.dimensions.com/element/sitting-male-side-1){#cubiclabel}
:::

# Typical seat height {.hiddenheading #sec-tsh}

According to [dimensions.com](https://www.dimensions.com), 115 [centizem](#cz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a zem"} ([cz](#cz){#centizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centizem"}) is the [typical seat height](https://www.dimensions.com/element/sitting-female-side-1#:~:text=Seat%20Height%20(Typical)%3A-,18%E2%80%9D%20%7C%2046%20cm,-Style%3A%20Casual) for both men and women in age range of 25 to 45 [y](#y){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"}. A box📦that is the size of a cubic [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} ([z&sup3;](#z3){#cubiczem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic zem"}) would likely fit under a typical chair or in between the shins of two people sitting in front of each other with their knees and feet 1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} apart and their legs🦵bent at right angles (25 [ct](#ct){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centiturn"}).

# Perpetually setting sun {.hiddenheading #sec-pss}

In [Slovak](https://sk.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zem)🇸🇰, [zem](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} means Earth. This is fitting because all Dec units are based on physical attributes of the Earth. At the Equator, the Earth rotates on its axis at a speed of [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1.00224 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"}. If we could indefinitely maintain this speed while flying West in an airplane✈️towards the setting sun️, we would be able to perpetually fly [into the sunset](https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/RidingIntoTheSunset)🌅.

# Airplane cruising speed {.hiddenheading #sec-acs}

To travel fast enough for a perpetual sunset, the airplane would need to surpass the [speed of sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_sound#:~:text=the%20distance%20travelled%20per%20unit%20of%20time%20by%20a%20sound%20wave)🔊([sos](#sos){#speedofsound .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}), which at 15 [°](#deg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"} [Celsius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Celsius#:~:text=the%20unit%20of%20temperature%20on%20the%20Celsius%20temperature%20scale) and 1 [standard atmosphere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_atmosphere_(unit)#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20pressure%20defined%20as%20101325%20Pa) is 0.735048 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} or Mach 1. [Mach numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mach_number) are relative to the [sos](#sos){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}, which varies greatly by air temperature and pressure. The cruising speed of a [Boeing 747](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boeing_747#:~:text=sweep%2C%20allowing%20a-,Mach%C2%A00.85,-%28490%C2%A0kn;%20900) is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.54 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} or Mach [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.85.

The highway🛣️speed of a car🚗is roughly tenfold slower than the cruising speed of an airplane️. If we are driving on a highway at a speed of 50 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"} and our exit is 1000 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} away, we will have 20 [centimillidays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundred thousandths of a day"} ([cmd](#cmd){#centimilliday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centimilliday"}) until we have to exit the highway️. To ensure we do not miss our exit, we can periodically check a countdown of the remaining [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}: \${zLeft}.

# Centimilliday (cmd) {.hiddenheading #sec-cmd}

Dec refers to [cmd](#cmd){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centimillidays"} as [beats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundred thousandths of a day"} ([b](#b){#beat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"}) because they are similar in duration to heart❤️beats or [musical beats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beat_(music)#:~:text=I-,n%20music%20and%20music%20theory%2C%20the%20beat%20is%20the%20basic%20unit%20of%20time,-%2C%20the). In Dec, 1 [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day"} = 100 [centiday]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a day"} ([cd](#cd){#centiday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centidays"}) = [10^5^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred thousand"} [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"} = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [microdays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millionths of a day"} ([µd](#ud){#microday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="microdays"}), 1 [mc](#mc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millitaur"} = 100 [kilozem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of zem"} ([kz](#kz){#kilozem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of zem"}) = [10^5^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred thousand"} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [decizem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a zem"} ([dz](#dz){#decizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizem"}) = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [nanotaurs]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millionths of a taur"} ([nc](#nc){#nanotaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaurs"}), and therefore, 1 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegar"} = $\text{mc}\over\text d$ = $\text {kz}\over\text {cd}$ = $\text z\over\text b$ = $\text {dz}\over\text{µd}$ = $\text {nc}\over\text{µd}$. A [cd]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centiday"} is [96]{.color960}% of a quarter hour and a [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beat"} is [86.4]{.color864}% of a second.

# Heart rate tempo {.hiddenheading #sec-hrt}

A [normal resting heart rate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heart_rate#:~:text=heart%20rate%20is-,60–100%20bpm,-.%20An%20ultra%2Dtrained) is between 100 and 166.[6]{.vinculum} [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"} per [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"} ([bpm](#bpm){#beatpermilliday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}). The unofficial anthem of the Dec measurement system, "[Turn the beat around](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turn_the_Beat_Around#:~:text=a%20disco%20song%20written%20by%20Gerald%20Jackson%20and%20Peter%20Jackson%2C%20and%20performed%20by%20American%20actress%20and%20singer%20Vicki%20Sue%20Robinson%20in%201976)", has a [tempo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempo#:~:text=the%20speed%20or%20pace%20of%20a%20given%20composition) of 188.64 [bpm](#bpm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}, which corresponds to the _allegro_ [tempo marking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempo#Approximately_from_the_slowest_to_the_fastest). A Dec clock⏰ticks at a rate of 100 [bpm](#bpm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}, $\text b^{-1}$, $1\over\text b$, 1 [inverse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplicative_inverse#:~:text=x%2C%20denoted%20by-,1/x%20or%20x%E2%88%921,-%2C%20is%20a%20number) beat, or 1 perbeat ([þ](#per){#perbeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeat"}), which is [1.15[740]{.vinculum}]{.colorIob} times more frequent than a [Hertz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hertz#:~:text=one%20event%20(or%20cycle)).

# Frequency period wavelength {.hiddenheading #sec-fpw}

Dec uses [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeat"}, [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"}, and [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, often with metric prefixes, to measure the [frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency#:~:text=the%20number%20of%20occurrences%20of%20a%20repeating%20event%20per%20unit%20of%20time), [period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency#:~:text=the%20reciprocal%20of%20the%20frequency), and [wavelength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelength#:~:text=the%20distance%20over%20which%20the%20wave%27s%20shape%20repeats), respectively, of a sound or light wave. Equations -@eq-freq, -@eq-peri, and -@eq-wave below show how frequency, period, and wavelength are related to each other and to speed. The speed of light ([sol](#sol){#speedoflight .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of light"}) is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}647.551657 [kiloomegars]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of omegars"} ([kv](#kv){#kiloomegar .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kiloomegars"}), which is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}881 thousand times faster than the [sos](#sos){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}.

:::{style="overflow-x:auto;overflow-y:hidden;"}
$$\text{frequency} = \dfrac{\text{speed}}{\text{wavelength}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{period}}$$ {#eq-freq}

$$\text{period} = \dfrac{\text{wavelength}}{\text{speed}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{frequency}}$$ {#eq-peri}

$$\text{wavelength} = \text{speed} \ast \text{period} = \dfrac{\text{speed}}{\text{frequency}}$$ {#eq-wave}
:::

The frequency range of the  [visible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_spectrum#:~:text=the%20band%20of%20the%20electromagnetic%20spectrum%20that%20is%20visible%20to%20the%20human%20eye) spectrum of light is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}345.6 to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}914.4 [teraperbeats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="trillions of perbeats"} ([Tþ](#Tpb){#teraperbeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="teraperbeats"}). The range of sound frequencies which can be [audible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=the%20frequency%20range%20that%20can%20be%20heard%20by%20humans)👂for humans is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[10.368](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=humans%20can%20hear%20sound%20as%20low%20as%2012%C2%A0Hz) to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[24192](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=8%5D%20and-,as%20high%20as%2028%C2%A0kHz,-%2C%20though%20the%20threshold) [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}. The period and wavelength that correspond to the frequency chosen by the range input below are 1000 &div; \${iobs} [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} = \${parseFloat((1000 / iobs).toFixed(3))} [millibeats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a beat"} ([mb](#mb){#millibeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millibeats"}) and 735.048 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"} &div; \${iobs} [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} = \${parseFloat((735.048 / iobs).toFixed(3))} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: iobinput
//| class: slider
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
viewof iobs = Inputs.range([1, 9999], { step: 1,  value: 380, label: "Frequency" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: beatinput
//| class: slider
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
viewof beats = Inputs.range([1, 999], { step: 1,  value: 1, label: "Duration" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: iobplayer
//| class: player
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
Play((t) => Math.sin(iobs / .864 * t * 2 * Math.PI), beats * .864, iobs)
```

# Decioctave octave note {.hiddenheading #sec-don}

In addition to [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}, the limits of human hearing can also be expressed in [decioctaves]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of an octave"} ([do](#do){#decioctave .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctaves"}). A [do](#do){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctave"} is a tenth of an [octave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octave#:~:text=an%20interval%20between%20two%20notes%2C%20one%20having%20twice%20the%20frequency%20of%20vibration%20of%20the%20other) ([o](#o){#octave .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="octave"}). An [o](#o){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="octave"} is a two-fold change in frequency. The audible range for humans is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[3]{.color3} to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[103]{.color3} [do](#do){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctaves"}. The [range](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Range_(music)#:~:text=the%20distance%20from%20the%20lowest%20to%20the%20highest%20pitch%20it%20can%20play) of an 88-key piano🎹is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}23.76 to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}3616.64 [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} or [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[8]{.color8} to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[80]{.color0} [do](#do){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctaves"}. The equations and code below convert between [o](#o){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="octaves"} and [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}.

$$\text{o} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{\text{p}}{14.1275}\right)$$ {#eq-octa}

$$\text{p} = 14.1275 \ast 2^{\text{o}}$$ {#eq-perb}

:::{.panel-tabset group="language"}
### Julia

```{julia}
perbeat(o) = 14.1275 * 2.0^o
octave(p) = log2(p / 14.1275)
octave(113.02)
perbeat(3)
```

### Observable JavaScript

```{ojs}
perbeat = o => 14.1275 * 2 ** o;
octave = p => Math.log2(p / 14.1275);
octave(113.02)
perbeat(3)
```

### Python

```{python}
import math
perbeat = lambda o: 14.1275 * 2 ** o
octave = lambda p: math.log2(p / 14.1275)
octave(113.02)
perbeat(3)
```

### R

```{r}
perbeat <- \(o) 14.1275 * 2^o
octave <- \(p) log2(p / 14.1275)
octave(113.02)
perbeat(3)
```

:::

# Color and sound {.hiddenheading #sec-cas}

Each [do](#do){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctave"} has a corresponding musical note ([n](#n){#note .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="musical note"}) that determines its color label. The idea of linking colors and musical notes dates back the [1704]{.color704} book by [Isaac Newton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Newton#:~:text=,an%20English%20polymath,-active%20as%20a) entitled [Optiks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opticks#:~:text=a%20collection%20of%20three%20books%20by%20Isaac%20Newton)[^0]. On [[2025]{.color025}[+]{.mono}[080]{.colorD080}]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="May 20, 2025"}, I read [The Color of Sound](https://www.flutopedia.com/sound_color.htm) by [Clint Goss](https://www.clintgoss.com)[^1], which presents a method of associating musical notes with colors by matching sound and light frequencies.

$$\text{n} = \text{o} \href{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modulo#:~:text=returns%20the%20remainder%20or%20signed%20remainder%20of%20a%20division}{\bmod} 1 \ast 10$$ {#eq-note}

The lollipops🍭in the chart below represent the ten Dec notes in Octave 4. The lollipops and the [do](#do){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decioctaves"} values above them are labeled with the ten Dec colors. Beneath each lollipop is its frequency rounded to the nearest [þ](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeat"}. Click or tap each lollipop to hear its associated sound. The chart demonstrates that data points can be labeled with both color and sound.

{{< include /dec/_lolli.qmd >}}

# Système international d'unités {.hiddenheading #sec-sid}

As a scientist of European origin, I have a strong preference for the [International System of Units](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_System_of_Units#:~:text=the%20world%27s%20most%20widely%20used%20system%20of%20measurement) ([SI](#si){#internationalsystemofunits .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"}) over the United States🇺🇸([US](#us){#unitedstates .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"}) [customary measurement system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_and_US_customary_measurement_systems). Nevertheless, having grown up in the [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"}, I understand the animosity towards unfamiliar measurement units expressed by Grandpa Simpson in "A Star Is Burns", Season 6 Episode 18 of [the Simpsons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Simpsons#:~:text=an%20American%20animated%20sitcom).

![](/asset/metric.jpg)

# US customary units {.hiddenheading #sec-ucu}

Dec redefines [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units to facilitate [conversion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conversion_of_units#:~:text=the%20conversion%20of%20the%20unit%20of%20measurement%20in%20which%20a%20quantity%20is%20expressed) with [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} and Dec units. The values in the first column of the tables below are approximate [fold changes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fold_change#:~:text=measure%20describing%20how%20much%20a%20quantity%20changes%20between%20an%20original%20and%20a%20subsequent%20measurement) from original to redefined [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units. A fold change of [1]{.color0} means [0]{.color0} [change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_change#:~:text=compare%20two%20quantities%20while%20taking%20into%20account%20the%20%22sizes%22%20of%20the%20things%20being%20compared) is required for alignment with the [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} and Dec units shown in the second and third column, respectively.

Unlike Dec and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"}, the [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary measurement system does not use metric prefixes to scale units by [powers of ten](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_of_10#:~:text=any%20of%20the%20integer%20powers%20of%20the%20number%20ten). Redefined [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units can serve as convenient reference points and provide intuitive names for certain fractions and multiples of Dec and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} units. For example, after being redefined, one hand is equal to a decimeter or a quarter [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}.

# Length and distance {.hiddenheading #sec-lad}

The [unidimensional](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/unidimensional#:~:text=not%20comparable%29-,one%2Ddimensional,-quotations%C2%A0%E2%96%BC) ([1D]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="unidimensional"}) units in @tbl-1d below can be divided into two groups: [human-](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_human-based_units_of_measurement#:~:text=units%20of%20measurement%20based%20on%20human%20body%20parts) or horse-based length units and [surveying](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surveying#:~:text=determining%20the%20terrestrial%20positions%20of%20points%20based%20on%20the%20distances%20and%20angles%20between%20them) distance units. A horse🐴length is about as long as a [cruiser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cruiser_(motorcycle)#:~:text=a%20motorcycle%20in%20the%20style%20of%20those%20made%20by%20American%20manufacturers) or [touring motorcycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Touring_motorcycle#:~:text=a%20type%20of%20motorcycle%20designed%20for%20touring)🏍️, approximately equivalent to the height of the tallest basketball🏀players, and roughly half of the length of a [compact](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compact_car#:~:text=sits%20between%20subcompact%20cars%20and%20mid%2Dsize%20cars) to [mid-size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mid-size_car#:~:text=larger%20than%20compact%20cars%20and%20smaller%20than%20full%2Dsize%20cars) car.

{{< include /dec/_lengthtable.qmd >}}

# Miles per hour {.hiddenheading #sec-mph}

When we divide a [1D]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="unidimensional"} unit by a time unit, we get a speed unit. A mile per hour is very close to a [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} per [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"} and a [knot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Knot_(unit)#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20speed%20equal%20to%20one%20nautical%20mile%20per%20hour), which is used to measure the speed of aircraft and watercraft, is almost exactly the same as 10/9 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} per [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"}. Dec refers to 10/9 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} as a [cubit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubit#:~:text=an%20ancient%20unit%20of%20length%20based%20on%20the%20distance%20from%20the%20elbow%20to%20the%20tip%20of%20the%20middle%20finger) or [ell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ell#:~:text=a%20northwestern%20European%20unit%20of%20measurement) ([ℓ](#ell){#cubit .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ell"}). An
 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegar"} can also be expressed as one [ℓ](#ell){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ell"} per Dec second ([s](#s){#second .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="Dec second"}). There are 86400 [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} seconds or 90000 [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="Dec seconds"} in one day.

{{< include /dec/_speedtable.qmd >}}

# Are hectare acre {.hiddenheading #sec-aha}

If we raise a [1D]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="unidimensional"} unit to the second power, we get a [bidimensional](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bidimensional#:~:text=not%20comparable%29-,Two%2Ddimensional,-.) ([2D]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="bidimensional"}) area unit. A [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} is 1 [hexamilliare]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="1.6 thousandths of an are"}, 16 square (sq.) [decimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a meter"} ([dm&sup2;](#dm2){#squaredecimeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square decimeters"}), [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.1975 Dec [sq. yards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_yard#:~:text=U.S.%20customary%20unit%20of%20area), 1.[7]{.vinculum} Dec [sq. feet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_foot#:~:text=the%20area%20of%20a%20square%20with%20sides%20of%201%20foot), or 256 Dec sq. inches. A sq. [kilozem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a thousand zem"} ([kz&sup2;](#kz2){#squarekilozem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square kilozem"}) is 1 [hexakilare]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="sixteen hundred ares"}, 16 [hectares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hectare#:~:text=a%20square%20with%20100%2Dmetre%20sides){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundreds of ares"}, 1600 [ares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hectare#Are), 40 Dec [acres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acre#:~:text=%20is-,a%20unit%20of%20land%20area,-used%20in%20the), 0.16 sq. [kilometers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometre#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20length%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of meters"} ([km&sup2;](#km2){#squarekilometer .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square kilometers"}), 0.0625 Dec [sq. miles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_mile#:~:text=US%20unit%20of%20measure%20for%20area), [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"}, or 1 megahexamilliare. 

{{< include /dec/_areatable.qmd >}}

# Drop wineglass keg {.hiddenheading #sec-dwk}

[US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} tridimensional ([3D]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tridimensional"}) volume units tend to scale by [powers of two](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_of_two#:~:text=a%20number%20of%20the%20form%202n%20where%20n%20is%20an%20integer). A cubic (cu.) [decizem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a zone equatorial meter"} ([dz&sup3;](#dz3){#cubicdecizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic decizem"}) is like a double shot, either of espresso☕️or of liquor🥃, and is equal to 1 cu. [nanotaur]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a millionth of a taur"} ([nc&sup3;](#nc3){#cubicnanotaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic nanotaur"}), 1 Dec wineglass (🍷glass), 2 Dec ounces ([u](#u){#ounce .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ounce"}), 4 Dec tablespoons (table🥄), 64 cu. [centimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundredth of a meter"} ([cm&sup3;]{#cubiccentimeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic centimeters"}), 64 [milliliters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a liter"} ([mL](#mL){#milliliter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliliters"}), 1000 Dec drops ([g](#g){#gutta .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="drops"}), or 1000 cu. [centizem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a zone equatorial meter"} ([cz&sup3;](#cz3){#cubiccentizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic centizem"}).

{{< include /dec/_volumetable.qmd >}}

# Grain pound ton {.hiddenheading #sec-gpt}

Dec and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} measurements of mass are based on volumes of water🌊. A [dz&sup3;](#dz3){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic decizem"} of water weighs $1\over7$ Dec pounds, 64 grams, or 1000 Dec grains ([g](#g){#grain .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grain"}). One [u](#u){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ounce"} of water weighs $1\over14$ Dec pounds, 500 Dec [g](#g){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grains"}, or 32 grams. In Dec, [g](#g){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grains"} is short for [*granum*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/granum) and [*gutta*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gutta), the Latin words for grain and drop, respectively. Similarly, [u](#u){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ounce"} originates from [*uncia*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/uncia), the Latin word for "a twelfth".

{{< include /dec/_masstable.qmd >}}

# Body mass index (bmi) {.hiddenheading #sec-bmi}

A [z&sup3;](#z3){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic zem"} is 1 keg. A keg of water weighs 64 [kilograms]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of grams"}, 128 Dec pounds, or a 1000 Dec [kilograins]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of grains"} ([kg](#kg){#kilograin .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"}). If [Leonardo da Vinci](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci#:~:text=an%20Italian%20polymath%20of%20the%20High%20Renaissance)'s [Vitruvian Man](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitruvian_Man#:~:text=a%20drawing%20by%20the%20Italian%20Renaissance%20artist%20and%20scientist%20Leonardo%20da%20Vinci) were 4 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} tall, we could measure 1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} from his knees to his feet or from his elbows to his fingertips. If he also weighed 1000 Dec [kg](#kg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"}, his [body mass index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_mass_index#:~:text=the%20body%20mass%20divided%20by%20the%20square%20of%20the%20body%20height) ([bmi](#bmi){#bodymassindex .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"}) would be 62.5 [kg](#kg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"} per [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} ($\text {kg}\over\text z^2$) or 25 kilograms per [m&sup2;](#m2){#squaremeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square meters"} ($\text {kilogram}\over\text m^2$).

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;"}
![](../asset/vitruvianMan.svg){#vitruvian}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Da_Vinci_Vitruve_Luc_Viatour_2.svg)
:::

A normal [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} ranges from 46.25 to 62.5 $\text {kg}\over\text z^2$ or 18.5 to 25 $\text {kg}\over\text m^2$. An obese person has a [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} above 75 $\text {kg}\over\text z^2$ or 30 $\text {kg}\over\text m^2$. Severe or morbid obesity is defined as a [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} above 100 $\text {kg}\over\text z^2$ or 40 $\text {kg}\over\text m^2$. A person with the weight and height selected by the range inputs below would be considered \${bmiStr}: \${kgrains} [kg](#kg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"} &div; \${zem2} [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} = \${bmi} $\text {kg}\over\text z^2$ = \${kgrams} kilograms &div; \${meter2} [m&sup2;](#m2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square meters"} = ${bmim2} $\text {kilograms}\over\text m^2$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| class: slider
//| label: kginput
viewof kilograins = Inputs.range([0, 10000], {label: "Weight", value: 1000, step: 1})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| class: slider
//| label: zinput
viewof zems = Inputs.range([0, 10], {label: "Height", value: 4, step: 0.01})
```

# Centizem centimeter inch {.hiddenheading #sec-cci}

The longest length depicted in the image of a ruler📏below is 1 [dz](#dz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizem"}, 1 [nc](#nc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaur"}, 4 [centimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a meter"}, or $8\over5$ Dec inches, and the shortest length is $1\over2$ [mz](#mz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millizem"}, $1\over5$ [millimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a meter"}, $1\over125$ Dec inches, or $1\over127$ [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary inches. A [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary inch is $127\over2$ [mz](#mz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millizem"}, $127\over5$ millimeters, or $127\over125$ Dec inches. A Dec inch is $5\over2$ centimeters. A centimeter is $5\over2$ [cz](#cz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centizem"}. A [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} is 4 decimeters or 16 Dec inches.

::: {.column-page-right fig-align="left" style="text-align:center;font-size:.825rem;"}
![](../asset/ruler1dz.svg){#ruler}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ruler_illustration.svg)
:::

# Claude Boniface Collignon {.hiddenheading #sec-cbc}

In [1788]{.color788}, [Claude Boniface Collignon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Boniface_Collignon#:~:text=a%20French%20attorney%20who%20contributed%20to%20scientific%20and%20social%20reforms%20in%20the%20time%20of%20the%20French%20Revolution) proposed measuring length in [dz](#dz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizem"} or [nc](#nc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaurs"} and tracking time in [xún](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_calendar#:~:text=into%20nine%2D%20or-,ten%2Dday%20weeks,-known%20as%20x%C3%BAn) ([x](#x){#xun .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="groups of ten days"}), [dd](#dd){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decidays"}, [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"}, [µd](#ud){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="microdays"}, and [nanodays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="billionths of a day"} ([nd](#nd){#nanoday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanodays"})[^2]. On [[2025]{.color025}[+]{.mono}[039]{.colorD039}]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 9, 2025"}, while searching for units akin to [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} and [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, I noticed the definition of a [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"}, 1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} = [10^-8^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred millionth"} [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} = 40 centimeters, in a table of [ten possible length units](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos/history/sm/node12.html) from an [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/physics/0412078) [article](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos/history/sm/sm.html) entitled "Why does the meter beat the second?"[^3].

# Summary {.unnumbered #tldr}

This article introduces the Dec measurement system and describes how Dec uses the properties of the planet Earth to define units based on turns for geographic coordinates, compass directions, distances, speeds, areas, volumes, weights, frequencies, wavelengths, and periods. Dec units can be combined with metric prefixes to form multiples or submultiples.

Dec attempts to bridge the gap, improve interoperability, and faciliate conversion between the [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} measurement systems by redefining certain units. Redefinition of [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units makes human-based length units [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[1.58]{.color0158}% shorter and surveying distance units [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[0.58]{.color0058}% shorter. Dec also redefines [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} seconds to be [4]{.color040}% shorter.

Dec color labels can convey an impression of a value at a glance👀. Dec sound labels allow us to quickly estimate a value without even having to look at it🙈. Both types of labels can help avoid confusion when [decimal separators](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decimal_separator#:~:text=In%20English%2Dspeaking%20countries%2C%20the%20decimal%20point%20is%20usually%20a%20small%20dot%20%28.%29%20placed%20either%20on%20the%20baseline) appear, disappear, or move due to a measurement unit change such as the addition, removal, or replacement of a metric prefix.

# Next {.unnumbered #next}

Now that you have had a taste👅of Dec, I hope that you are hungry🤤for more! If so, dive🤿deeper by reading my article on Dec [dates](/dec/date), [times](/dec/time), and [snaps](/dec/snap). My [filter](/quarto/filter) and [include](/quarto/include) articles discuss the [Quarto](/quarto) publishing system and how I customize my Quarto website to display a Dec [snaps](/dec/snap) in the [navigation bar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navigation_bar#:~:text=a%20section%20of%20a%20graphical%20user%20interface%20intended%20to%20aid%20visitors%20in%20accessing%20information) and Dec [dates](/dec/date) in the [article list](/list) and [title blocks](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/title-blocks).

:::{#firstnav}
{{< include /asset/_decquanav.qmd >}}
:::

[^0]: [Newton, Issac](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Newton#:~:text=,an%20English%20polymath,-active%20as%20a). [1704]{.color704}. *Opticks*. <https://doi.org/10.5479/sil.302475.39088000644674>.
[^1]: [Clint Goss](https://www.clintgoss.com). [2022+098]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 7, 2022"}. *Color of Sound*. <https://www.flutopedia.com/sound_color.htm>.
[^2]: [Collignon, Claude Boniface](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Boniface_Collignon#:~:text=a%20French%20attorney%20who%20contributed%20to%20scientific%20and%20social%20reforms%20in%20the%20time%20of%20the%20French%20Revolution). 1788. *Découverte d'étalons justes, naturels, invariables et universels*. <https://archive.org/details/dcouvertedtalon00collgoog/page/n68/mode/2up>.
[^3]: [Agnoli, Paolo](http://www.paoloagnoli.it) & [D'Agostini, Giulio](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos). [2004+330]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 25, 2005"}. *Why does the meter beat the second?*. <https://arxiv.org/abs/physics/0412078>.

# Observable notebooks {.unnumbered #ojs}

In alphabetical order below, you will find a list of the [Observable](http://observablehq.com) [computational notebooks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notebook_interface#:~:text=a%20virtual%20notebook%20environment%20used%20for%20literate%20programming) that inspired many of the visualizations above and thus deserve their own section before the [references](#references) and [footnotes](#footnotes) further below. On the [Observable website](https://observablehq.com), you can [search](https://observablehq.com/search) for other awesome Observable notebooks, read the [blog](https://observablehq.com/blog), or watch [webinars](https://observablehq.com/resource-center#webinars) and [other videos](https://observablehq.com/resource-center#videos).

::: {.column-page-right #citelist}
00. [Armstrong, Zan](https://observablehq.com/@zanarmstrong). [2023+057]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 27, 2023"}. *Text color annotations in markdown*. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/text-color-annotations-in-markdown>.
01. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock). [2020+335]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 30, 2021"}. *Time Zones*. <https://observablehq.com/@mbostock/time-zones>.
02. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock). [2022+037]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 7, 2022"}. *Solar Terminator*. <https://observablehq.com/@d3/solar-terminator>.
03. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock). [2023+314]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 9, 2024"}. *Input: Table*. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/input-table>.
04. [Edwards, Paul](https://observablehq.com/@pjedwards). [2022+171]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="August 19, 2022"}. *Compass Rose as legend with colors*. <https://observablehq.com/@pjedwards/compass-rose-as-legend-with-colors>.
05. [Freedman, Dylan](https://observablehq.com/@freedmand). [2017+345]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="February 9, 2018"}. *Sounds*. <https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds>.
06. [Gordon, Marcus A.](https://observablehq.com/@magfoto). [2018+288]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="December 14, 2018"}. *Wavelengths and Spectral Colours*. <https://observablehq.com/@magfoto/wavelengths-and-spectral-colours>.
07. [Harmath, Dénes](https://observablehq.com/user/@thsoft). [2018+104]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 13, 2018"}. *ABC*. <https://observablehq.com/@thsoft/abc>.
08. [Johnson, Ian](https://observablehq.com/@enjalot). [2021+121]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 30, 2021"}. *Draggable World Map Coordinates Input*. <https://observablehq.com/@enjalot/draggable-world-map-coordinates-input>.
09. [Lim, Maddie](https://observablehq.com/@maddievision). [2018+330]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 25, 2019"}. *Enneagram*. <https://observablehq.com/@maddievision/enneagram>.
10. [Paavanb](https://observablehq.com/@paavanb). [2024+006]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="March 7, 2024"}. *Progressive Color Picker*. <https://observablehq.com/@paavanb/progressive-color-picker>.
11. [Patel, Amit](https://observablehq.com/@redblobgames). [2021+290]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="December 16, 2021"}. *Compass Rose*. <https://observablehq.com/@paavanb/progressive-color-picker>.
12. [Pettiross, Jeff](https://observablehq.com/@pettiross). [2024+150]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="July 19, 2024"}. *Categorical color scheme test tool*. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/categorical-palette-tool>
13. [Rieder, Lukas](https://observablehq.com/@lukasrieder). [2023+032]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 2, 2023"}. *Editable table*. <https://observablehq.com/@parlant/editable-table>.
